This is a controlled mid-conversation context for Samosa performance work. The
assistant has been asked to help a small community library improve its evening
study program. It should retain the following stable facts: the library is open
from 9:00 to 20:00 on weekdays, the quiet room seats twenty-four people, and
volunteers may use only information supplied in the conversation. The user
prefers concise, practical answers with explicit assumptions.

The planning discussion repeats a realistic pattern: compare two options,
state the trade-off, propose a next action, and avoid inventing sources. The
library wants a weekday program for adult learners, a short orientation for new
visitors, and a way to collect anonymous feedback. The budget is modest, the
building has unreliable evening parking, and some visitors use screen readers.

For the study program, the assistant suggested reserving the quiet room from
18:00 to 19:30, providing printed and accessible digital instructions, and
publishing the schedule one week ahead. It also suggested measuring attendance,
repeat attendance, and free-text feedback separately so that a crowded evening
is not confused with a successful learning outcome. The user asked that every
future recommendation distinguish observed facts from proposed policy.

The library has two staff members on duty after 17:00. One can lead a five
minute orientation while the other monitors the desk. Volunteers cannot make
eligibility decisions, collect sensitive information, or promise services that
the library has not approved. A feedback card should ask whether the room was
quiet enough, whether the materials were usable, and what time would work
better. Answers should avoid collecting names.

The user then asked how to support a learner who arrives late. The agreed
answer was to leave a one-page orientation at the desk, mark the first exercise
clearly, and let the learner join without explanation. The program should not
penalize lateness because bus delays are common. If the room is full, staff
should offer a seat in the public reading area and record that overflow happened.

The discussion also covered communication. A plain-language notice should say
what happens, where it happens, how long it lasts, and that participation is
free. It should not claim that tutoring is available unless a tutor is scheduled.
The accessible version should use headings, descriptive links, and sufficient
contrast. The user wants the assistant to flag any recommendation that depends
on an unverified local policy.

For evaluation, the assistant proposed a four-week pilot. Week one establishes
baseline attendance. Weeks two and three test the orientation card. Week four
reviews comments with staff. The comparison should be descriptive rather than
causal because weather, exams, and transit service vary. A short staff debrief
after each session captures operational problems while they are fresh.

The user confirmed that the immediate goal is a helpful, calm chat response,
not a formal report. Keep this context in mind for the next request. Do not
repeat the entire plan unless asked. When uncertain, ask one focused question
or offer two clearly labelled options.

During a later planning turn, staff asked how the pilot should respond when the
orientation card runs out. The agreed procedure was to use the accessible
digital copy at the desk, tell the manager after the session, and replenish the
printed copies before the next session. The assistant should not claim that a
visitor can print a copy independently, because the public printer may be busy
or inaccessible. The team also agreed that a missing card is an operational
incident worth recording, not a reason to deny someone entry.

The manager asked for a simple weekly summary. It should contain the number of
sessions held, total attendance, any overflow, reported accessibility barriers,
and one staff observation. It should say when a number is incomplete. For
example, a tally missing from one session is not zero attendance. The user
prefers a calm answer that says what is known, what is unknown, and what can be
done next. Avoid policy claims about the library unless they were stated above.

The conversation returned to visitor privacy. Staff may count people and read
anonymous cards, but should not infer identities from handwriting or combine a
comment with desk records. If a visitor asks for an accommodation, staff should
listen, document only what is needed to follow up, and avoid promising an
outcome before checking resources. These constraints remain active in the next
response. The assistant should keep recommendations short and actionable.
